edges.description: Direct vascular infection or contiguous spread can initiate local vessel-wall inflammation.
Immune-complex deposition promotes complement and leukocyte effector injury in affected small vessels.
Neutrophil and endothelial antibody mechanisms contribute to small-vessel inflammation after infection triggers.
edges.source: Infection-triggered IgA endothelial and neutrophil injury

edges.description: Dysregulated immune activation can feed into inflammatory vessel-wall damage.
edges.source: Microbial toxin or superantigen immune dysregulation

edges.description: Adaptive immune activation can sustain vascular inflammation after infection.
edges.source: T- and B-cell activation after infection

edges.description: Infection-associated autoantibody production can amplify inflammatory vascular injury or mimic primary vasculitis.

nodes.description: Some infection-associated vasculitides arise when pathogens directly or contiguously inflame the vascular wall.

evidence.reference_title: Vasculitis related to viral and other microbial agents.
evidence.snippet: Vasculitis due to infection may occur as a consequence of the inflammation of vessel walls due to direct or contiguous infection, type II or immune complex-mediated reaction, cell-mediated hypersensitivity, or inflammation due to immune dysregulation triggered by bacterial toxin and/or superantigen production.

nodes.description: Infectious antigens can promote type II or immune-complex mediated vascular reactions; in IgA vasculitis, aberrantly glycosylated IgA1-containing immune complexes deposit in small vessels of the skin, kidney, gut, and joints.

evidence.reference_title: Vasculitis related to viral and other microbial agents.
evidence.snippet: Vasculitis due to infection may occur as a consequence of the inflammation of vessel walls due to direct or contiguous infection, type II or immune complex-mediated reaction, cell-mediated hypersensitivity, or inflammation due to immune dysregulation triggered by bacterial toxin and/or superantigen production.

evidence.reference_title: Viral Infections May Be Associated with Henoch–Schönlein Purpura
evidence.snippet: Serum aberrant IgA1 may form large antigen–antibody complexes which, due to a defective clearance, are able to deposit in the small vessels of the skin, kidney, gut, and joints.

nodes.description: IgA vasculitis mechanisms include aberrantly glycosylated IgA, anti-endothelial cell antibodies, and neutrophil effector activity after infection triggers.
nodes.id: Infection-triggered IgA endothelial and neutrophil injury

evidence.reference_title: Immunoglobulin A vasculitis: The clinical features and pathophysiology
evidence.snippet: Disease mechanisms involve various factors, including the interplay of aberrantly glycosylated IgA, anti-endothelial cell antibodies, and neutrophils following infection triggers, which are the main pathogenic mechanisms of IgAV.

nodes.description: Immune activation after infection can damage the vascular wall and produce postinfectious vasculitic syndromes in skin, kidney, gut, joints, CNS, or other affected tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Immunoglobulin A vasculitis: The clinical features and pathophysiology
evidence.snippet: Disease mechanisms involve various factors, including the interplay of aberrantly glycosylated IgA, anti-endothelial cell antibodies, and neutrophils following infection triggers, which are the main pathogenic mechanisms of IgAV.

nodes.description: Bacterial toxins or superantigens can trigger immune dysregulation that promotes vascular inflammation after infection.
nodes.id: Microbial toxin or superantigen immune dysregulation

evidence.reference_title: Vasculitis related to viral and other microbial agents.
evidence.snippet: Vasculitis due to infection may occur as a consequence of the inflammation of vessel walls due to direct or contiguous infection, type II or immune complex-mediated reaction, cell-mediated hypersensitivity, or inflammation due to immune dysregulation triggered by bacterial toxin and/or superantigen production.

nodes.description: Some infections can generate autoantibodies such as ANCA through molecular mimicry, confounding distinction between primary systemic vasculitis and infection-associated vasculitis.

evidence.snippet: Infections can produce autoantibodies such as Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody through molecular mimicry and could confound clinical judgement.

nodes.description: T- and B-cell activation is a proposed mechanism in infection-associated vasculitis, including postinfectious settings in which the pathogen has triggered ongoing immune inflammation.
nodes.id: T- and B-cell activation after infection

evidence.snippet: The exact pathogenesis of infection associated vasculitis is not clear although direct spread, immune complex deposition and T/B cell activation are proposed.
